This document details the implementation of a new ‘Smart Grid Initiative’ focused on enhancing energy distribution and resilience within the state of California. Specifically, the project targets the modernization of existing power lines and substations, utilizing advanced sensor technology and data analytics. The initiative is spearheaded by the California Energy Commission (CEC) and the Department of Defense (DoD), with significant involvement from the California Public Utilities Commission (CPUC) and the Federal Energy Management Agency (FEMA). The primary goal is to reduce energy losses during transmission, improve grid stability, and facilitate the integration of renewable energy sources, ultimately contributing to a more sustainable and reliable energy infrastructure across the state. The project encompasses a phased approach, beginning with a comprehensive assessment of current infrastructure and culminating in the deployment of smart grid technologies.

The project, initiated in 2018, focused on developing a new AI-powered diagnostic tool for early detection of Alzheimer’s disease. Initially presented as a significant investment, the project faced significant challenges related to data acquisition and algorithm accuracy. Despite initial optimistic projections, the tool ultimately failed to achieve the desired level of performance and was subsequently cancelled after a year of development and testing.
